The Verge Tech News — Text Mining & Topic Modelling

Group 01

2026-04-24

1 Introduction

This project performs text mining and topic modelling on technology news articles scraped from The Verge. We collect article titles, authors, dates, and full body text from the archive, then apply natural language processing (NLP) techniques including tokenization, lemmatization, TF-IDF, and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) to discover key themes and trends in tech journalism.

2 Setup & Libraries

packages <- c(
  "textstem", "rvest", "purrr", "dplyr", "stringr", "stringi", "topicmodels",
  "tm", "SnowballC", "tidytext", "ggplot2", "wordcloud", "RColorBrewer"
)
install.packages(packages)
library(textstem)
library(rvest)
library(purrr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(stringi)
library(topicmodels)
library(tm)
library(SnowballC)
library(tidytext)
library(ggplot2)
library(wordcloud)
library(RColorBrewer)

3 Data Collection (Web Scraping)

3.1 Text Cleaning Function

Before scraping, we define a cleaning function that standardises text encoding, removes URLs, punctuation, and extra whitespace.

clean_text <- function(x) {
  x %>%
    iconv(from = "latin1", to = "UTF-8", sub = "") %>%
    stri_trans_general("latin-ascii") %>%
    str_to_lower() %>%
    str_replace_all("https?://\\S+", "") %>%
    str_replace_all("[[:punct:]]", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("[\r\n]", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("[^[:print:]]", " ") %>%
    str_squish()
}

3.2 Scrape Archive Pages

We scrape The Verge tech archive pages (pages 1–30), extracting article titles, links, dates, and authors.

get_verge_page <- function(page_num) {
  base_url <- "https://www.theverge.com"
  url  <- paste0(base_url, "/tech/archives/", page_num)
  page <- tryCatch(read_html(url, encoding = "UTF-8"), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (is.null(page)) return(NULL)

  titles  <- page %>% html_elements("div.hp1qhq3 :not(span) > a") %>% html_text2()
  links   <- page %>% html_elements("div.hp1qhq3 :not(span) > a") %>% html_attr("href")
  dates   <- page %>% html_elements("div.hp1qhq3 time")            %>% html_attr("datetime")
  authors <- page %>% html_elements("div.hp1qhq3 span span span span") %>% html_text2()

  n <- min(length(titles), length(links), length(dates), length(authors))
  if (n == 0) return(NULL)

  tibble(
    Title  = titles[1:n],
    Link   = links[1:n],
    Date   = dates[1:n],
    Author = authors[1:n]
  )
}

page_range   <- 1:30
results_list <- list()

for (i in page_range) {
  cat("Scraping page", i, "\n")
  tmp <- get_verge_page(i)
  if (!is.null(tmp)) results_list[[length(results_list) + 1]] <- tmp
}
## Scraping page 1 
## Scraping page 2 
## Scraping page 3 
## Scraping page 4 
## Scraping page 5 
## Scraping page 6 
## Scraping page 7 
## Scraping page 8 
## Scraping page 9 
## Scraping page 10 
## Scraping page 11 
## Scraping page 12 
## Scraping page 13 
## Scraping page 14 
## Scraping page 15 
## Scraping page 16 
## Scraping page 17 
## Scraping page 18 
## Scraping page 19 
## Scraping page 20 
## Scraping page 21 
## Scraping page 22 
## Scraping page 23 
## Scraping page 24 
## Scraping page 25 
## Scraping page 26 
## Scraping page 27 
## Scraping page 28 
## Scraping page 29 
## Scraping page 30
result <- bind_rows(results_list)
cat("Total raw rows scraped:", nrow(result), "\n")
## Total raw rows scraped: 361

3.3 Scrape Article Body Text

For each article link we scrape the full body text and apply the cleaning function.

get_article_text <- function(url) {
  tryCatch({
    page       <- read_html(url, encoding = "UTF-8")
    paragraphs <- page %>%
      html_elements("div.duet--article--article-body-component p") %>%
      html_text2()
    clean_text(paste(paragraphs, collapse = " "))
  }, error = function(e) NA)
}

4 Data Cleaning

4.1 Filter & Standardise Links

We remove rows with missing fields, fix relative URLs, and keep only links pointing to The Verge domain.

base_url <- "https://www.theverge.com"

results <- result %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(Title),  Title  != "",
    !is.na(Link),   Link   != "",
    !is.na(Date),   Date   != "",
    !is.na(Author), Author != "",
    !str_detect(Link, "^#")
  ) %>%
  mutate(Link = if_else(str_detect(Link, "^https?://"), Link, str_c(base_url, Link))) %>%
  distinct(Link, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  filter(str_detect(Link, "^https?://([a-zA-Z0-9-]+\\.)?theverge\\.com"))

# Remove placeholder author "and"
results1 <- results %>% filter(Author != "and")

4.2 Fetch Article Text & Clean Fields

results1$ArticleText <- map_chr(results1$Link, possibly(get_article_text, NA_character_))

results1 <- results1 %>%
  mutate(
    Title       = clean_text(Title),
    Author      = clean_text(Author),
    ArticleText = clean_text(ArticleText)
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(ArticleText), ArticleText != "")

cat("Articles with text:", nrow(results1), "\n")
## Articles with text: 127

4.3 Clean Dates

results1 <- results1 %>%
  mutate(Date_clean = suppressWarnings(as.Date(Date))) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Date_clean)) %>%
  mutate(Date = Date_clean) %>%
  select(-Date_clean)

cat("Date range:", as.character(min(results1$Date)),
    "to", as.character(max(results1$Date)), "\n")
## Date range: 2026-01-26 to 2026-04-23

4.4 Preview Cleaned Dataset

head(results1 %>% select(Title, Author, Date))
## # A tibble: 6 × 3
##   Title                                                        Author Date      
##   <chr>                                                        <chr>  <date>    
## 1 leak reveals new xbox game pass a starter editiona thata s … tom w… 2026-04-23
## 2 claude is connecting directly to your personal apps like sp… stevi… 2026-04-23
## 3 scrapped microsoft gaming in favor of xbox                   tom w… 2026-04-23
## 4 all hands meeting                                            rober… 2026-04-23
## 5 pro                                                          tom w… 2026-04-23
## 6 horizon tv hub                                               tom w… 2026-04-23

5 Tokenization & Lemmatization

We tokenize the article text into individual words, remove standard and custom stop words, keep only alphabetic tokens, and then lemmatize each word (reduce to its base form).

custom_stopwords <- c("ita", "verge", "tech", "said", "will", "youa", "ia", "thata", "wea")

tokens <- results1 %>%
  select(Link, ArticleText) %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, ArticleText) %>%
  filter(!word %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  filter(!word %in% custom_stopwords) %>%
  filter(str_detect(word, "^[a-z]+$")) %>%
  mutate(word = lemmatize_words(word))

cat("Total tokens after cleaning:", nrow(tokens), "\n")
## Total tokens after cleaning: 39900

6 Word Frequency Analysis

6.1 Top 20 Most Frequent Words

word_freq <- tokens %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  top_n(20, n)

ggplot(word_freq, aes(x = reorder(word, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top 20 Words in The Verge Articles",
       x = "Word", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()

6.2 Word Cloud (Top 80 Words)

custom_word_freq <- tokens %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  top_n(80, n)

wordcloud(
  words        = custom_word_freq$word,
  freq         = custom_word_freq$n,
  min.freq     = 2,
  max.words    = 500,
  random.order = FALSE,
  colors       = brewer.pal(8, "Dark2")
)

7 TF-IDF Analysis

TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) measures how important a word is to a specific article relative to all articles. High TF-IDF words are distinctive to that article.

tfidf <- tokens %>%
  count(Link, word) %>%
  bind_tf_idf(word, Link, n)

# Top 5 TF-IDF words per article, joined with titles
top_tfidf <- tfidf %>%
  group_by(Link) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 5) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  left_join(results1 %>% select(Link, Title), by = "Link") %>%
  mutate(Title_short = str_trunc(Title, 40))

# Sample: top ~12 articles
tfidf_sample <- top_tfidf %>%
  group_by(Title_short) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 5) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 60)

ggplot(tfidf_sample, aes(x = reorder_within(word, tf_idf, Title_short),
                          y = tf_idf, fill = Title_short)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ Title_short, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top TF-IDF Words per Article",
       x = "Word", y = "TF-IDF Score") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8)

8 Document-Term Matrix

We build a Document-Term Matrix (DTM) where each row is an article and each column is a word. This is required for LDA topic modelling.

dtm <- tokens %>%
  count(Link, word) %>%
  cast_dtm(document = Link, term = word, value = n)

cat("DTM dimensions:", dim(dtm)[1], "documents x", dim(dtm)[2], "terms\n")
## DTM dimensions: 127 documents x 6715 terms

9 Topic Modelling (LDA)

Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is an unsupervised machine learning technique that discovers hidden topics in a collection of documents. Each topic is a probability distribution over words.

We set k = 6 topics.

set.seed(123)
k         <- 6
lda_model <- LDA(dtm, k = k, control = list(seed = 123))

lda_terms <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "beta")   # word-topic probabilities
lda_docs  <- tidy(lda_model, matrix = "gamma")  # doc-topic probabilities

9.1 Top Terms per Topic

Each bar shows the top 10 words most associated with each topic (by beta probability).

top_terms <- lda_terms %>%
  group_by(topic) %>%
  slice_max(beta, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  arrange(topic, -beta)

ggplot(top_terms, aes(x = reorder_within(term, beta, topic),
                       y = beta, fill = factor(topic))) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ topic, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_reordered() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Top Terms per LDA Topic",
       x = "Term", y = "Beta (Probability)") +
  theme_minimal()

9.2 Most Common Topics Across Articles

This chart shows how many articles are primarily associated with each topic.

doc_topics <- lda_docs %>%
  group_by(document) %>%
  slice_max(gamma, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(doc_topics, aes(x = factor(topic))) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(title = "Most Common Topics Across Articles",
       x = "Topic", y = "Count of Articles") +
  theme_minimal()

9.3 Word Clouds per Topic

A word cloud for each of the 6 LDA topics, where word size reflects the beta probability.

for (i in 1:k) {
  terms_topic <- lda_terms %>%
    filter(topic == i) %>%
    arrange(desc(beta))

  wordcloud(
    words        = terms_topic$term,
    freq         = terms_topic$beta,
    max.words    = 100,
    colors       = brewer.pal(8, "Dark2"),
    random.order = FALSE,
    main         = paste("Topic", i)
  )
  title(paste("Topic", i))
}

10 Trend Analysis

10.1 Number of Articles Published Over Time

trend_data <- results1 %>% count(Date)

ggplot(trend_data, aes(x = Date, y = n)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Trend of Articles Published Over Time",
       x = "Date", y = "Number of Articles") +
  theme_minimal()

11 Save Outputs

The cleaned dataset, TF-IDF scores, and DTM are saved as CSV files. Update the paths below to match your local machine.

write.csv(results1,          "clean_news2.csv",   row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(tfidf,             "tfidf_verge.csv",   row.names = FALSE)
write.csv(as.matrix(dtm),    "dtm_verge.csv")

cat("Saved", nrow(results1), "records to clean_news2.csv\n")
cat("Saved TF-IDF data to tfidf_verge.csv\n")
cat("Saved DTM to dtm_verge.csv\n")

12 Conclusion

This project scraped and analysed technology news articles from The Verge using text mining and NLP techniques. Key findings include:

  • The most frequent terms across all articles reflect dominant tech topics such as AI, software, devices, and companies.
  • TF-IDF analysis revealed that each article has a distinctive vocabulary that differentiates it from others.
  • LDA topic modelling successfully identified 6 latent topics, capturing themes such as consumer electronics, AI/software, policy, and media.
  • Publication trends show variation in article volume over the scraped time period.

This type of analysis is valuable for understanding media focus, tracking emerging tech trends, and building recommendation or classification systems.
